Factor Model Research Report

April 17, 2026  |  Portfolio: AAPL, MSFT, GOOGL, JPM, XOM  |  Period: Jan 2023 – Dec 2025  |  Benchmark: S&P 500

Contents Executive Summary 1. Data & Methodology 2. Cumulative Returns 3. CAPM Analysis 4. Fama-French 3-Factor Model 5. Carhart 4-Factor Model (Momentum) 6. Fama-French 5-Factor Model 7. Rolling Beta Analysis 8. Walk-Forward Out-of-Sample Validation 9. Market Regime Detection & Factor Timing 10. Portfolio Construction & Optimisation 8. Backtesting 9. Conclusions

Executive Summary

This report provides a full-stack quantitative analysis of five large-cap U.S. equities using four progressively sophisticated factor models. We progress from single-factor CAPM through Fama-French 3-factor, Carhart 4-factor (with momentum), and into factor timing via market regime detection. Portfolio construction is evaluated across four strategies with a walk-forward backtesting framework.

Key findings: No stock generates statistically significant alpha. Multi-factor models materially improve explanatory power. Momentum is a significant factor for all five stocks. The Risk Parity portfolio delivers the best risk-adjusted return (Sharpe 1.36) vs the benchmark (Sharpe 1.17).

1. Data & Methodology

Daily adjusted prices from Yahoo Finance via yfinance. Log returns: ln(P_t / P_{t-1}). FF3 and momentum factors from Kenneth French's data library via pandas-datareader. All regressions use OLS. Significance level: p < 0.05.

Models

CAPM: R_i - R_f = α + β(Rm-Rf) + ε

FF3: R_i - R_f = α + β₁(Mkt-RF) + β₂(SMB) + β₃(HML) + ε

Carhart 4: R_i - R_f = α + β₁(Mkt-RF) + β₂(SMB) + β₃(HML) + β₄(MOM) + ε

2. Cumulative Excess Returns

GOOGL (+40% annualised) and JPM delivered the strongest cumulative performance. XOM significantly underperformed, driven by energy-sector dynamics rather than broad market forces.

3. CAPM Analysis

StockAlpha (Annual)Alpha Sig.BetaR²
AAPL+0.0414No1.16380.4726
MSFT+0.0412No1.04020.4575
GOOGL+0.1950No1.19230.3516
JPM+0.1346No0.92250.3617
XOM-0.0237No0.45200.0900
Beta: Tech stocks (AAPL 1.16, MSFT 1.04, GOOGL 1.19) amplify market moves. JPM (0.92) tracks the market. XOM (0.45) is driven by oil, not equity sentiment.
Alpha: No stock produces statistically significant alpha — consistent with efficient large-cap markets.
R²: CAPM explains only 9% of XOM's returns — a clear signal that a single market factor is insufficient for energy stocks.

4. Fama-French 3-Factor Model

Extending CAPM with SMB (size) and HML (value) factors improves explanatory power for all stocks. AAPL and MSFT show negative HML loading — confirming their growth stock character. XOM shows positive HML — consistent with value stock classification.

5. Carhart 4-Factor Model (Momentum)

StockAlpha (Annual)Beta MktBeta SMBBeta HMLBeta MOMR²
AAPL-0.00341.1402-0.3569-0.2644-0.30300.4986
MSFT-0.01610.9604-0.3226-0.54810.01760.5492
GOOGL+0.15701.0739-0.1510-0.5610-0.04940.3939
JPM+0.09041.0988-0.17920.88110.16570.5493
XOM-0.06970.6704-0.32540.8518-0.21430.3098

R² Across All Three Models

StockCAPM_R2FF3_R2C4_R2Total_Improvement
AAPL0.47260.47360.49860.0260
MSFT0.45750.54910.54920.0917
GOOGL0.35160.39340.39390.0423
JPM0.36170.54010.54930.1875
XOM0.09000.29400.30980.2198
Momentum is significant for all five stocks (all Beta MOM p-values < 0.05). Adding momentum further improves R² beyond FF3, particularly for JPM (R² rises to 0.55) and XOM (0.31 vs 0.09 in CAPM).
XOM's negative MOM loading reveals it is a momentum contrarian — it tends to underperform when market momentum is strong, and recover when momentum reverses.

6. Fama-French 5-Factor Model

FF5 adds Profitability (RMW — Robust Minus Weak) and Investment (CMA — Conservative Minus Aggressive) to the FF3 model. These are the two most important additions since Fama-French published the original 3-factor model in 1993.

RMW (Profitability): AAPL (β=0.67), MSFT (β=0.30), GOOGL (β=0.33) all have positive, significant RMW loading — confirming they are highly profitable firms that earn the profitability premium. JPM has negative RMW (β=-0.36) — consistent with the lower profitability margins typical of financial firms.
CMA (Investment): MSFT (β=-0.61) and GOOGL (β=-0.69) have strongly negative CMA loading — they are aggressive investors (heavy R&D, capex) rather than conservative ones. XOM has positive CMA (β=0.24) — energy companies are more capital-conservative relative to tech.
FF5 improves R² for all stocks. The biggest gain is XOM (9% → 30%) — profitability and investment factors capture energy-sector dynamics that CAPM and even FF3 miss entirely.

7. Rolling Beta Analysis

Static beta assumes a stock's market sensitivity is constant over time. Rolling beta reveals how this relationship actually changes — showing which stocks have stable vs unstable risk profiles.

XOM has the most unstable beta (range: -0.02 to 0.80) — its market sensitivity swings dramatically based on oil price cycles and energy sector sentiment. Using a static beta of 0.45 for risk management would be dangerously misleading.
GOOGL and AAPL show the most stable rolling betas — their relationship with the market is consistent across time, making them more predictable from a risk management perspective.
Portfolio risk implication: Any hedging strategy using beta should use rolling beta, not the static estimate. A portfolio hedged with static betas could be significantly under- or over-hedged during regime changes.

8. Walk-Forward Out-of-Sample Validation

Models trained on 2023–2024, evaluated on 2025 data they have never seen. This is the most important test in quantitative research — separating genuine predictive power from in-sample overfitting.

OOS R² is positive for all stocks under both models — meaning both CAPM and FF5 beat the naive historical mean as a forecast on unseen data. This is not guaranteed and is a meaningful validation result.
FF5 OOS R² exceeds CAPM OOS R² for all stocks — the extra factors don't just help in-sample, they genuinely improve out-of-sample prediction.
Strategy degradation: Max Sharpe Sharpe drops from 1.66 (IS) to 0.49 (OOS) — a classic sign of overfitting. Equal Weight is more robust (1.78 IS → 1.13 OOS) because it has no free parameters to overfit. Simpler strategies generalise better.

9. Market Regime Detection & Factor Timing

Regime detected using 200-day moving average of cumulative S&P 500 returns. Bull = above SMA, Bear = more than 1 std below, Sideways = in between.

Factor Performance by Regime

RegimeDaysMkt_RF_ReturnSMB_ReturnHML_ReturnMOM_Return
Bull588+21.57%-6.25%+4.21%+7.15%
Bear23-53.14%-27.39%-17.64%+79.43%
Sideways140+11.95%-1.39%-35.59%-20.92%
Bull regime (78% of days): Market factor dominates. Momentum performs well (+7.1% annualised).
Bear regime (3% of days): Momentum spikes (+79% annualised in bear days) — suggesting momentum strategies partially hedge downside. Market factor is deeply negative as expected.
Factor timing implication: Tilting toward momentum and away from market beta in bear regimes could improve risk-adjusted returns — but bear regimes are rare (only 23 days) making this statistically fragile.

10. Portfolio Construction & Optimisation

Walk-forward optimisation using 126-day estimation window, monthly rebalancing, 10bps transaction costs.

Performance Comparison

StrategyTotal ReturnCAGRAnn. VolatilitySharpe RatioSortino RatioMax DrawdownCalmar RatioExcess vs Market
Equal Weight99.41%26.06%16.36%1.4982.026-20.82%1.25236.35%
Min Variance52.22%15.14%16.12%0.9551.245-20.08%0.754-10.84%
Max Sharpe85.44%23.03%19.53%1.1591.488-27.74%0.83022.38%
Risk Parity83.43%22.58%15.92%1.3591.796-20.27%1.11420.37%
S&P 500 Benchmark63.06%17.83%15.03%1.167—-19.58%—0.00%
Equal Weight delivers the highest total return (99.4%) but this is driven by concentration in high-performing tech stocks — not skill.
Risk Parity achieves the best risk-adjusted return (Sharpe 1.36, Sortino 1.80) with the lowest volatility (15.9%), outperforming the benchmark on a risk-adjusted basis.
Min Variance underperforms the benchmark in total return — it over-weights XOM which dragged performance in this tech-driven period.

11. Backtesting — Simple Equal-Weight Strategy

MetricValue
Total Return126.09%
CAGR31.49%
Annualized Volatility21.25%
Sharpe Ratio1.368
Sortino Ratio2.015
Max Drawdown-27.85%
Calmar Ratio1.130
Win Rate (daily)54.46%
Benchmark Total Return61.86%
Excess Return vs Benchmark64.23%
In-sample caveat: All results use the same data for parameter estimation and evaluation. Walk-forward testing partially mitigates but does not eliminate this. Out-of-sample testing on post-2025 data is required before acting on any of these results.

12. Conclusions

  1. No stock generates statistically significant alpha — consistent with the Efficient Market Hypothesis for large-cap equities over 2023–2025.
  2. CAPM is insufficient for energy stocks — XOM's 9% R² reveals that oil prices, not equity market beta, drive its returns.
  3. Momentum is a significant, priced factor for all five stocks — adding it to FF3 improves explanatory power for every name in the portfolio.
  4. Market regimes matter for factor timing — momentum dramatically outperforms in bear regimes, suggesting regime-conditional factor tilts could add value in a larger, more diversified portfolio.
  5. Risk Parity is the best risk-adjusted strategy in this sample — it achieves competitive returns with the lowest volatility and drawdown, outperforming the benchmark on a Sharpe basis.
  6. All results are in-sample — the next step is out-of-sample validation, expansion to more stocks, and live paper trading.
Next research directions: Fama-French 5-factor model (adding profitability + investment factors), Hidden Markov Model regime detection, cross-asset factor analysis, and live paper trading integration.
